docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Postman is one of the most widely used tools for exploring, testing, and debugging RESTful APIs. This user guide introduces the essential features of Postman and provides step‑by‑step instructions for performing common API testing tasks. It is designed for beginners who are new to API development but already understand the basic concepts of REST.

Whether you are validating backend endpoints, experimenting with HTTP requests, or learning how client–server communication works, this guide will help you build confidence using Postman in a real development workflow.

docs.title: Intended Users
docs.text:
This guide is written for:

  • Students learning web development or backend fundamentals
  • Beginners who want to practice sending HTTP requests
  • Developers who need a simple tool to test RESTful APIs
  • Anyone using Postman for the first time and looking for a structured introduction

This document assumes no prior experience with Postman itself.

docs.title: Prerequisite Knowledge
docs.text:
Before using this guide, readers should already understand:

  • The basic structure of a RESTful API (resources, endpoints, HTTP verbs, request/response cycle)
  • Common HTTP methods: GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
  • Basic JSON formatting
  • How to install desktop applications on their operating system

No programming experience is required, but it will be helpful when working with more advanced examples.

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
To follow the tasks in this guide, users must have:

  • Postman Desktop Application (Windows, macOS, or Linux — latest stable version recommended)
  • A stable internet connection
  • Access to at least one API endpoint for testing (public APIs or your own backend project)

Optional but recommended:

  • A code editor (e.g., Visual Studio Code)
  • A browser with developer tools (Chrome, Firefox, Edge)

docs.title: Overview of Tasks
docs.text:
This guide is organized into several hands‑on tasks that introduce Postman’s core features:

  1. Task 1 — Sending a Basic GET Request Learn how to send your first request and read the response.

  2. Task 2 — Working with Query Parameters and Headers Understand how to modify requests and inspect server behavior.

  3. Task 3 — Sending POST Requests with JSON Bodies Practice creating and sending data to an API.

  4. Task 4 — Using Collections to Organize API Requests Learn how to save, group, and reuse requests efficiently.

Each task builds on the previous one, allowing beginners to gradually develop confidence using Postman.

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text:
This guide uses the following conventions to improve clarity:

  • Bold text — UI elements, important terms
  • Italic text — Emphasis or new concepts
  • Monospace — Code, JSON, URLs, and command-line text

Note

This is a helpful tip or reminder.

Warning

This is an important caution to prevent errors.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In this section, you will send a GET request to the Postman Echo API.

This section is organized into three parts:

  • Build the Request — Create a new request, set the method, enter the URL, and configure the query parameters, body, and headers (Steps 1–6).
  • Send & Review — Send the request and read the response (Steps 7–8).
  • Save the Request — Save the request to a collection for future use (Step 9).

By the end of this section, you will know how to send a real HTTP request and understand what the response tells you.

What is a GET request?

A GET request asks a server to send you data. It is the most common type of HTTP request — like asking a website to show you a page.

What is Postman Echo?

Postman Echo is a simple API provided by Postman for testing HTTP requests.

docs.title: Build the Request
docs.text: This section covers Steps 1–6: creating the request, choosing the method, entering the URL, and configuring the params, body, and headers.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create a New Request
docs.text:
Click the + button at the top of the Postman workspace to open a new request tab.

  1. Open the Postman desktop app.
  2. Click the + button at the top of the screen to open a new tab.
  3. A blank request editor opens.

Info

You can also press Ctrl+T (Windows / Linux) or Cmd+T (macOS) to open a new tab.

docs.title: Step 2 — Set the Method to GET
docs.text:
The method dropdown is on the left side of the URL bar. Confirm it shows GET.

  1. Find the method dropdown on the left side of the URL bar.
  2. By default, Postman sets every new request to GET.
  3. Confirm it shows GET. If not, click the dropdown and select GET.

Common HTTP methods

Method What it does GET Retrieve data from the server POST Send new data to the server PUT Replace existing data PATCH Update part of existing data DELETE Remove data

docs.title: Step 3 — Enter the URL
docs.text:
Type the base URL into the address bar. Do not add query parameters yet.

  1. Click the URL bar (it shows placeholder text like Enter URL or paste text).
  2. Type the following URL:
https://postman-echo.com/get

Warning

Do not add ?foo1=bar1&foo2=bar2 to the URL yet. You will add query parameters in Step 4 using the Params tab instead. This keeps your request easier to manage.

docs.title: Step 4 — Add Query Parameters
docs.text:
Query parameters let you pass extra information along with your request. They appear at the end of a URL, after a ?:

https://postman-echo.com/get?foo1=bar1&foo2=bar2

Instead of typing parameters directly into the URL, use the Params tab. Postman updates the URL automatically.

Add key-value pairs in the Params tab. Postman builds the URL for you.

  1. Click the Params tab below the URL bar.
  2. In the first empty row, enter:
  3. Key: foo1
  4. Value: bar1
  5. In the next row, enter:
  6. Key: foo2
  7. Value: bar2
  8. Look at the URL bar — it now shows:
https://postman-echo.com/get?foo1=bar1&foo2=bar2

Info

Each row in the Params tab has a checkbox. When a checkbox is unchecked, that parameter is not sent. This is useful for testing with and without a parameter without deleting it.

docs.title: Step 5 — Check the Request Body
docs.text:
For GET requests, the body should always be set to none.

  1. Click the Body tab below the URL bar.
  2. Confirm none is selected.
  3. Leave it as is — no body is needed for this request.

Info

GET requests do not send a body. Body data is used with POST, PUT, and PATCH requests when you need to send data to the server — for example, a new user's name and email in JSON format.

docs.title: Step 6 — Add a Request Header
docs.text:
Headers give the server extra information about your request — for example, what format you want the response in.

Add the Accept header in the Headers tab. Postman auto-fills suggestions as you type.

  1. Click the Headers tab below the URL bar.
  2. You will see some headers already listed in grey. These are added automatically by Postman and are always sent.
  3. In the first empty row, add:
  4. Key: Accept
  5. Value: application/json

Info

The Accept header tells the server that you want the response in JSON format. For this practice API, the server returns JSON regardless — but setting this header is good practice for working with real APIs.

Info

As you type in the Key field, Postman shows suggestions like Content-Type and Accept. Click a suggestion to fill it in automatically.

docs.title: Send & Review
docs.text: This section covers Steps 7–8: sending the request and reading what comes back.

docs.title: Step 7 — Send the Request
docs.text:
Click the blue Send button. Postman sends your request to the server.

  1. Click the blue Send button on the right side of the URL bar.
  2. Postman sends the request to https://postman-echo.com/get?foo1=bar1&foo2=bar2.
  3. Wait a moment — the response appears in the bottom half of the screen.

docs.title: Step 8 — Read the Response
docs.text:
After you click Send, the Response panel appears at the bottom of the screen. Check the status code first, then the body.

Status Code

The status code appears at the top of the response panel. 200 OK means success.

Look at the top of the response panel. You should see:

200 OK

This means the request worked. The server received your request and sent data back.

Success

If you see 200 OK, your request worked correctly. Move on to read the response body below.

Common status codes

Code Meaning 200 OK — the request worked 400 Bad Request — something is wrong with your request 401 Unauthorized — you need to provide credentials 404 Not Found — the URL does not exist 500 Server Error — something went wrong on the server 
Hover over the status code in Postman to read a short description of what it means.

Response Body

Click the Body tab in the response panel to see the data the server sent back.

  1. In the Response panel (bottom half of the screen), click the Body tab.
  2. You should see JSON data like this:
{
  "args": {
    "foo1": "bar1",
    "foo2": "bar2"
  },
  "headers": {
    "host": "postman-echo.com",
    "accept": "application/json"
  },
  "url": "https://postman-echo.com/get?foo1=bar1&foo2=bar2"
}

What does this mean?

Field What it shows args The query parameters you sent: foo1 and foo2 headers The headers your request included, including Accept url The full URL of your request 
If you see `"foo1": "bar1"` and `"foo2": "bar2"` inside `args`, everything worked correctly.

Other Response Tabs

Tab What it shows Body The data the server sent back Cookies Any cookies returned by the server Headers Response headers sent by the server Test Results Pass/fail results if you added test scripts

docs.title: Step 9 — Save the Request
docs.text:
Saving lets you open and resend the request any time without setting it up again.

Click Save or press Ctrl+S / Cmd+S to open the Save Request dialog.

  1. Click the Save button at the top right of the request editor, or press Ctrl+S (Windows / Linux) or Cmd+S (macOS).
  2. A Save Request dialog opens.

Enter a name for the request and choose a collection to save it in.

  1. Fill in the details:
  2. Request name: GET - Query Params
  3. Collection: Click New Collection, name it My First Collection, and click Create. Or select an existing collection.
  4. Click Save.

Your request now appears in the left sidebar under your collection. Click it any time to reload it.

What is a Collection?

A collection is a folder that holds related requests together. You can organise, share, and run all requests in a collection at once. Think of it as a playlist for your API requests.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text:
By the end of this section, you will have successfully learned the following:

  • How to create and configure a GET request in Postman.
  • How to add query parameters, headers, and check the body.
  • How to read and understand the server response.
  • How to save a request to a collection.

Congratulations 🎉 You sent your first API request!

docs.title: Content Tabs
docs.text: Content tabs let you group related content under selectable labels — ideal for showing the same instructions in multiple languages, or comparing alternative approaches side by side.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true # required for the modern tab style
  - pymdownx.superfences # needed for code blocks inside tabs

docs.title: Basic Tabs
docs.text:
Wrap tab content with === "Label" blocks. Indentation must be consistent (4 spaces).

=== "Python"
`python
    import httpx
    response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")
    `

=== "JavaScript"
`js
    const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/users");
    const data = await res.json();
    `

=== "cURL"
`bash
    curl https://api.example.com/v1/users \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
    `

Result:

Python

python import httpx response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")

JavaScript

js const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/users"); const data = await res.json();

cURL

bash curl https://api.example.com/v1/users \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"

docs.title: Tabs with Text and Lists
docs.text:
Tab content isn't limited to code — you can include any Markdown inside a tab.

=== ":material-apple: macOS"
Install via Homebrew:

    1. Open Terminal
    2. Run `brew install mkdocs`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

=== ":material-linux: Linux"
Install via pip:

    1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
    2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

=== ":material-microsoft-windows: Windows"
Install via pip in PowerShell:

    1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
    2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

Result:

:material-apple: macOS

Install via Homebrew:

1. Open Terminal
2. Run `brew install mkdocs`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`
:material-linux: Linux

Install via pip:

1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`
:material-microsoft-windows: Windows

Install via pip in PowerShell:

1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

docs.title: Linked Tabs
docs.text:
By default, all tab groups on a page switch independently. To sync tabs with the same label across the whole page, add slugify to your config:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true
      slugify: !!python/object/apply:pymdownx.slugs.slugify
        kwds:
          case: lower

When a reader selects "Python" in one group, every other tab group on the page that has a "Python" tab will switch to it automatically.

docs.title: Setting up Emojis
docs.text: Material for MkDocs supports the full set of Twemoji and Material Design Icons via a simple shortcode syntax. Once configured, you can drop emojis and icons anywhere in your Markdown.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add the following to your mkdocs.yml:

markdown_extensions:
  - attr_list
  - pymdownx.emoji:
      emoji_index: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.twemoji
      emoji_generator: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.to_svg

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Use :name: shortcodes inline with your text:

:smile: :rocket: :warning: :white_check_mark: :tada:

Result: :smile: :rocket: :warning: :white_check_mark: :tada:

docs.title: Common Emoji Shortcodes
docs.text: Shortcode Emoji Use case :rocket: 🚀 Launches, deploys :white_check_mark: ✅ Success, completed :warning: ⚠️ Caution, deprecation :x: ❌ Errors, unsupported :bulb: 💡 Tips, ideas :book: 📖 Documentation, reading :lock: 🔒 Security, auth :gear: ⚙️ Configuration, settings

docs.title: Emoji in Headings and Lists
docs.text:
Emojis work inside headings, list items, and tables — anywhere Markdown is rendered.

## :rocket: Deployment

- :white_check_mark: Step one complete
- :gear: Configuring environment
- :lock: Securing credentials

Result:

docs.title: :rocket: Deployment
docs.text:
• :white_check_mark: Step one complete
  • :gear: Configuring environment
  • :lock: Securing credentials

docs.title: Finding Emojis
docs.text: Browse the full list at emojipedia.org or search the Twemoji cheat sheet. Most standard emoji names work as-is with underscores replacing spaces.

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text: Material for MkDocs renders Mermaid.js diagrams natively. Write diagrams as fenced code blocks — no plugins or image exports needed.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.superfences:
      custom_fences:
        - name: mermaid
          class: mermaid
          format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format

docs.title: Flowchart
docs.text:
Use flowcharts to illustrate processes, decision trees, and request lifecycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Client] --> B{Auth?}
  B -- Yes --> C[API Server]
  B -- No  --> D[401 Unauthorized]
  C --> E[(Database)]
  C --> F[200 Response]
```

Result:

flowchart LR
  A[Client] --> B{Auth?}
  B -- Yes --> C[API Server]
  B -- No  --> D[401 Unauthorized]
  C --> E[(Database)]
  C --> F[200 Response]

docs.title: Sequence Diagram
docs.text:
Sequence diagrams show message exchanges between actors over time — perfect for documenting authentication flows and API handshakes.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  participant C as Client
  participant A as Auth Service
  participant S as API Server

  C->>A: POST /oauth/token
  A-->>C: access_token
  C->>S: GET /v1/data (Bearer token)
  S->>A: Validate token
  A-->>S: Token valid
  S-->>C: 200 OK { data }
```

Result:

sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  participant C as Client
  participant A as Auth Service
  participant S as API Server

  C->>A: POST /oauth/token
  A-->>C: access_token
  C->>S: GET /v1/data (Bearer token)
  S->>A: Validate token
  A-->>S: Token valid
  S-->>C: 200 OK { data }

docs.title: Entity Relationship Diagram
docs.text:
Use ERDs to document data models and database schemas.

```mermaid
erDiagram
  USER {
    uuid   id PK
    string email
    string name
  }
  COLLECTION {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   owner_id FK
    string name
  }
  REQUEST {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   collection_id FK
    string method
    string url
  }

  USER        ||--o{ COLLECTION : owns
  COLLECTION  ||--o{ REQUEST    : contains
```

Result:

erDiagram
  USER {
    uuid   id PK
    string email
    string name
  }
  COLLECTION {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   owner_id FK
    string name
  }
  REQUEST {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   collection_id FK
    string method
    string url
  }

  USER        ||--o{ COLLECTION : owns
  COLLECTION  ||--o{ REQUEST    : contains

docs.title: State Diagram
docs.text:
State diagrams are useful for documenting lifecycle states — webhooks, job queues, order statuses, and more.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
  [*]      --> Pending
  Pending  --> Running  : job starts
  Running  --> Success  : completes
  Running  --> Failed   : error
  Failed   --> Pending  : retry
  Success  --> [*]
```

Result:

stateDiagram-v2
  [*]      --> Pending
  Pending  --> Running  : job starts
  Running  --> Success  : completes
  Running  --> Failed   : error
  Failed   --> Pending  : retry
  Success  --> [*]

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Mermaid diagrams adapt automatically to light and dark mode.
  • Keep diagrams small and focused — one concept per diagram is easier to read.
  • For complex architecture diagrams, consider splitting into multiple focused diagrams rather than one large one.
  • Full Mermaid syntax reference: mermaid.js.org

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
I see "Could not send request" or a network error : Check your internet connection. Make sure the URL is exactly https://postman-echo.com/get.

The response body is empty or nothing appears : Make sure you clicked the Body tab in the bottom (response) panel — not the Body tab in the top (request) panel.

My parameters are missing from the response args : Go to the Params tab and confirm the checkboxes next to foo1 and foo2 are checked. Unchecked rows are not sent with the request.

I see a status code other than 200 : Hover over the status code in Postman to see what it means. Double-check your URL for any typos.
